stem: If \( f([[a]])=[[fa]] \) and \( |f'(x)|\le [[M]] \), the MVT gives the largest possible \( f([[b]]) \). What is that bound?

Suppose \( f([[a]])=[[fa]] \) and \( f'(x)\ge [[m]] \) for all \( x \) in \( \left[[[a]],[[b]]\right] \) (a one-sided bound, not an absolute value). By the Mean Value Theorem, what is the smallest possible value of \( f([[b]]) \)?
solution: By the MVT, some \( c\in([[a]],[[b]]) \) satisfies \( f'(c)=\dfrac{f([[b]])-f([[a]])}{[[b]]-[[a]]}\ge [[m]] \), so \( f([[b]])\ge f([[a]])+[[m]]([[b]]-[[a]])=[[answer]] \); this is the smallest value the theorem allows.
distractor_rationales: This subtracts instead of adds, giving an upper-bound-style expression for a lower bound.
This multiplies by \( f(a) \) instead of adding to it.
This is off by one from the correct bound.
This is off by one from the correct bound.

stem: Suppose \( f([[a]])=[[fa]] \) and \( |f'(x)|\le M \) for all \( x \) in \( \left[[[a]],[[b]]\right] \). For what smallest value of \( M \) does the Mean Value Theorem guarantee \( f([[b]])\le [[T]] \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses \( b \) alone instead of \( b-a \) in the denominator.
This adds \( f(a) \) instead of subtracting it.
This omits the division by \( b-a \) entirely.
This omits \( f(a) \) from the numerator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x)=x^{2} \) on the interval \( \left[[[a]],[[b]]\right] \). The Mean Value Theorem guarantees some \( c \) in \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[b]])-f([[a]])}{[[b]]-[[a]]} \). What is that value of \( c \)?
solution: The average rate of change simplifies to \( \dfrac{b^{2}-a^{2}}{b-a}=a+b \), so \( 2c=[[a]]+[[b]] \Rightarrow c=\dfrac{[[a]]+[[b]]}{2}=[[answer]] \), which indeed lies in \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the division by 2 (forgets to solve \( 2c=a+b \) for \( c \)).
This confuses \( c \) with the interval length \( b-a \).
This computes half the interval length rather than the midpoint of \( a,b \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
